hook: Punch cartoonist's untouched Aesthetic-era townhouse, frozen since 1875
facts: Home of Punch illustrator Edward Linley Sambourne from 1875 to 1910
Crammed with Morris & Co. wallpapers, stained glass and blue-and-white china
Kept unaltered by his son until 1946, then saved by the Victorian Society
Reopened by the council in 2022 after a long restoration
w: Edward Linley Sambourne drew for Punch and never threw anything out. His Kensington terrace, redecorated in 1875 in full Aesthetic clutter of Morris wallpaper, stained glass and blue-and-white china, stayed untouched after his death; his son kept it as a shrine. The best-preserved Victorian home anywhere. A house that forgot to move on.

hook: Tiny Victorian graveyard of beloved dogs behind a park lodge
facts: Begun around 1881 in the garden of Victoria Lodge, a park keeper's house
Roughly 1,000 animals buried before it largely closed in 1903
Headstones about the size of a house-brick, with names and epitaphs
Owned by The Royal Parks; viewable through the railings, tours occasional
w: Behind Victoria Lodge, off Bayswater Road, sit hundreds of headstones no bigger than house-bricks. A park keeper began burying pets here around 1881, starting with a Maltese named Cherry; some thousand dogs, cats, monkeys and birds followed before it closed. Peer through the railings at the saddest little necropolis in London.

hook: Art Nouveau tyre depot studded with Bibendum stained glass
facts: Built as Michelin's UK HQ and tyre depot, opened January 1911
Designed by Michelin engineer Francois Espinasse, not an architect
Bibendum stained glass lost in WWII, later remade from old drawings
Street-level tiles depict racing cars that ran on Michelin tyres
w: Michelin's UK depot, opened 1911 and drawn by one of their own engineers, turned tyre-fitting into pageantry: jolly Bibendum glaring out of stained glass, racing cars rendered in coloured tiles, a former fitting bay now a smart restaurant. The wartime windows vanished and were remade from old posters. Art Nouveau, in rubber.

hook: 1950s coffee house where Dylan played a London cellar
facts: Opened 1954 by Michael and Sheila van Bloemen as a bohemian cafe
Cellar became a cornerstone of the British folk revival
Bob Dylan played here in 1962 under the name Blind Boy Grunt
One of the last original coffee houses of its era still trading
w: Pete Seeger told a young Bob Dylan to find Anthea at the Troubadour; in 1962 he turned up and played the cellar as Blind Boy Grunt. Open since 1954, this Old Brompton Road coffee house kept the British folk revival alive downstairs while bohemians argued upstairs. Coffee, candlelight and a sticky little stage.

hook: Medieval merchant's hall moved stone by stone from the City
facts: Great Hall built 1466 as Crosby Place in Bishopsgate, City of London
Once leased and bought by Thomas More, who held a Chelsea estate nearby
Moved five miles to Chelsea in 1910 to escape demolition
Only surviving medieval City merchant house, now part of a private home
w: London's only surviving medieval merchant's hall sits in the wrong place on purpose. Raised in Bishopsgate in 1466 and once held by Thomas More, the Great Hall faced demolition in 1910, so it was dismantled into 1,500 numbered pieces and rebuilt by the Chelsea river. Now wrapped inside a billionaire's private house.

hook: London's tallest church spire, looming over Kensington
facts: Present church built 1872 by Sir George Gilbert Scott
Spire of 278 feet, the tallest in London, finished after Scott's death
Design echoes St Mary Redcliffe in Bristol
Reached from the High Street through a long Victorian cloister
w: The tallest church spire in London hides in plain sight above Kensington High Street: 278 feet of Gilbert Scott neo-Gothic, finished only after he died. Reach it through a long, hushed Victorian cloister that swallows the traffic noise. Modelled on Bristol's St Mary Redcliffe, and quietly outsoaring everything around it.

hook: Squatted Victorian church turned Carnival-born arts centre
facts: Built 1887 as the evangelical Talbot Tabernacle, a red-brick Romanesque pile
Squatted and occupied in 1973 amid local campaigns for community space
Steel bands rehearsed here ahead of Notting Hill Carnival
Now a theatre, gallery, studios and garden bar on a peppercorn lease
w: A curved red-brick Romanesque church went up on Powis Square in 1887; by 1973 squatters had taken it and locals were demanding it as a community hall. Carnival steel bands rehearsed inside, and it became the cultural engine of Notting Hill. Now theatre, gallery and garden bar, held on a peppercorn rent. The people won this one.

hook: Bombed Jacobean mansion, half ruin half garden theatre
facts: Jacobean Cope Castle built 1605 by diplomat Sir Walter Cope
Gutted by incendiary bombs on the night of 27/28 September 1940
Only the east wing and parts of the south facade survive
Open-air opera now plays against the surviving arcaded wall
w: Cope Castle went up in 1605 and grew into the grandest salon in Whig London, until incendiaries gutted it one night in September 1940. Only the east wing and a skeletal arcade survived. Each summer opera is staged against that roofless wall while peacocks stalk the lawns. Faded grandeur with a stage set built from ruin.

hook: Faded 1898 playhouse turned cinema turned wild little theatre
facts: Opened 1898 as a theatre designed by W. G. R. Sprague
Ellen Terry and Sarah Bernhardt trod its boards early on
Became a cinema by 1923 and stayed one for decades
Now The Coronet Theatre, with a studio named the Print Room
w: Sprague built this Notting Hill Gate playhouse in 1898; Bernhardt and Ellen Terry played it before the talkies turned it into a cinema for seventy years. The faded gilt and worn velvet never got tidied up, which is exactly the appeal. Now a fearless little theatre that leaves the peeling grandeur on show. Beautifully unrestored.

hook: Lone bottle kiln, last relic of England's oldest stoneware works
facts: Pottery founded 1672 by John Dwight by Putney Bridge
Dwight the earliest clearly documented maker of stoneware in England
Works demolished 1975; one brick bottle kiln deliberately kept
Grade II listed and now ringed by later development on New King's Road
w: John Dwight set up his stoneware works by Putney Bridge in 1672, the first documented English stoneware maker. The pottery ran for three centuries, then fell to redevelopment in 1975, with a single brick bottle kiln spared. It stands marooned among newer blocks on New King's Road. One stubborn chimney where an industry began.

hook: Early Gothic Revival church where Dickens married
facts: Designed by James Savage in 1819, among the earliest true Gothic Revival churches
Stone-vaulted nave carried on real flying buttresses, rare for its date
Charles Dickens married Catherine Hogarth here on 2 April 1836
A Commissioners' church, Grade I listed, just off the King's Road
w: Two days after the first Pickwick Papers appeared, Charles Dickens married Catherine Hogarth here, in 1836. James Savage's 1819 church gambled on real stone vaulting and flying buttresses decades before Gothic Revival went mainstream. Tall, pale and earnest off the King's Road. A wedding venue with a stone ceiling that actually holds.

hook: River church with Thomas More's surviving Tudor chapel
facts: South chapel rebuilt 1528 as Sir Thomas More's private chapel
Pillar capitals reputedly designed by Hans Holbein the Younger
Mostly destroyed by a parachute mine in April 1941, rebuilt on old foundations
Reconsecrated 1958, the More Chapel having weathered the blast best
w: Thomas More rebuilt the south chapel here in 1528, with capitals supposedly drawn by Holbein, and worshipped a short walk from his Chelsea house. A parachute mine flattened most of the church in 1941, yet More's chapel rode out the blast. Rebuilt stone by stone on its old footprint by the river. Tudor survival, rescued from rubble.

hook: White-tiled wallpaper factory, a proto-Modernist landmark
facts: Built 1902 by C. F. A. Voysey for Sanderson's wallpaper firm
Clad in white glazed brick banded with Staffordshire blue
Once linked to the main factory by a footbridge over Barley Mow Passage
Grade II* listed, an early step toward Modern Movement design
w: Voysey drew wallpapers for Sanderson before designing their factory, and in 1902 gave Chiswick a building decades ahead of itself: crisp white glazed brick banded in blue, all clean verticals, a footbridge once leaping the passage to the works opposite. Historians call it a hinge toward Modernism. A wallpaper shed that changed buildings.

hook: Westwood's punk shop with a 13-hour clock spinning backwards
facts: Westwood and McLaren ran it as Let It Rock, SEX and Seditionaries from 1971
SEX (1974-76) outfitted the nascent Sex Pistols scene
Relaunched as Worlds End in 1979 with a slanting, galleon-like floor
A 13-hour clock above the door still runs backwards
w: At the unfashionable end of the King's Road, Vivienne Westwood and Malcolm McLaren ran the shop that birthed punk, trading as Let It Rock, then SEX, then Seditionaries. Reborn as Worlds End in 1979 with a tilting galleon floor and a 13-hour clock whirling backwards over the door. Still Westwood's, time still running the wrong way.
